summary: DHH announces that 37signals received over 2,200 applications for a junior programmer position, all reviewed by humans without AI screening. He pushes back against the narrative that AI has eliminated the need for junior developers, arguing that humans who understand code will always be necessary to vet AI-generated work. He also notes that landing a job isn't about meeting a checklist of criteria but about being the best candidate in the applicant pool.
key_points: 37signals received 2,200+ applications for one junior role, all reviewed by hand without AI screening
Getting a job isn't about meeting a fixed checklist — it's about being the best candidate relative to everyone else who applied
The narrative that the industry no longer needs junior programmers is wrong
Even as AI improves, humans who understand code will always be needed to vet machine-generated work
Great cover letters that don't read like AI slop are a baseline requirement
money_quotes: We received over 2,200 applications for our just-closed junior programmer opening, and now we're going through all of them by hand and by human. No AI screening here.
Yes, you have to present a baseline of relevant markers to even get into consideration, like a great cover letter that doesn't smell like AI slop.
To actually get the job, you have to be the best of the ones who've applied!
Almost every job opening is grading applicants on the curve of everyone who has applied. And the best candidate of the lot gets the job. You can't quantify what that looks like in advance.
It would be great to help counter the narrative that this industry no longer needs juniors.
However good AI gets, we're always going to need people who know the ins and outs of what the machine comes up with.
It's truly utopian thinking that mankind won't need people capable of vetting the work done by AI in five minutes.
key_insight: No matter how capable AI becomes, the industry will always need junior programmers who can grow into people capable of understanding and vetting what the machines produce.
